title: The Accepted Token
description:
Amaka hands you a captured JWT that The Griot used successfully against Sankofa's legacy admin API. The audit team wants to know what the root cause was.

  eyJhbGciOiJub25lIiwidHlwIjoiSldUIn0.eyJzdWIiOiJhZG1pbiIsInJvbGUiOiJzdXBlcl9hZG1pbiIsImlhdCI6MTcwMDAwMDAwMH0.

Decode it yourself first if you need to — the tools are on your machine. Then pick the root cause. Be careful: three of the options describe real JWT weaknesses, but only one describes this specific token.

choices: The header specifies alg=none, which some JWT libraries accept as a valid signature algorithm meaning no signature is verified at all — the server trusted the unsigned claims.
The signature is missing entirely, which any compliant JWT verifier would reject, so the root cause must be a logic bug elsewhere in the server.
The token uses HS256 with a weak shared secret that the attacker guessed, which is why the signature on the third segment verifies.
The token claims role=super_admin — the server should never have accepted that claim for a user whose account is only admin.
